title: RA.1004 dj sweet6teen

translation.content: "What kind of music do I actually want to play?" Every artist asks themselves this at some point in their career. What is a sound? And why do we personally identify with it? For Lea Lang, AKA dj sweet6teen, this question is the guiding force behind her RA Mix. Born in Aachen, a German spa city close to the border with Belgium, Lang found her musical feet in the vibrant student hub of Cologne. It was while studying social work at the Technische Hochschule that she fell into the nightlife scene. Finding her sound wasn't an instant process. Lang cut her teeth on breaks-heavy house and prog (think Angel D'Lite), traces of which you can hear peppered across RA.1004. But as she explains in the interview below, the pandemic years were, musically, a turning point. "High BPMs and short-lived trends became very dominant and I realised I couldn't stand the pace anymore," she writes. "That's when my sound naturally shifted into something more minimalist and timeless." Nowadays, you'll find Lang in Berlin during the week, and on weekends… well, take your pick. A busy touring schedule means she's on the road almost constantly —this summer she's debuted at Horst Arts & Music, Butik, Dimensions and Panorama Bar (to name just a few). And her RA Mix? It oozes sultry charm from the jump. Buoyant with gyrating low-end, it's hard to think her sound was ever anything else: vocals twirl around analogue basslines, material à la Eddie Richards and Terry Francis's historic Wiggle parties, as well the kind of bongo action that wouldn't feel out of place in an Apollonia session. Call it waft, wiggle, smooch house—whatever it is, we like it. – Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? To be very honest, I've been going through quite a deep personal journey. I actually came out of a depressive episode that, for a moment, made me think I couldn't play music anymore: my hearing felt different; I totally lost my flow and intuition; I struggled with social anxiety. It was a really tough phase, but now, I've managed to overcome it. That's why this mix feels like such an achievement, almost like proof to myself I can still do it, that the music and the connection are still there. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This is my first mix in two and a half years that I've consciously curated and built from start to finish. It follows the idea to rediscover many of the records from my collection that helped me figure out my true sound spectrum and answer the question: what kind of music do I actually want to play? So these tracks carry a lot of emotional weight for me. But there are also some freshly dug ones included. In a way, this mix shows my very own definition of house music: techy, sometimes dubby, a little bit of prog and breaks, but always groovy and… sexy? The whole thing was recorded at the studio of The Mudd Show in Kreuzberg, Berlin. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Right now, I'm really looking forward to playing more shows with a clear head and fresh energy. After everything I've been through, it feels special to enjoy the process again: digging for records and connecting with people on the dance floor. I'm also excited to keep growing (my label), Phantasy Dance, and to invite more inspiring artists sharing their sound. As soon as I finish university, I'd love to explore music production and develop a vision for my own record label. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Mental health is a cause I care deeply about. Having experienced difficult times in the past, I've seen how much pressure in the music and nightlife scene can take a toll, so raising awareness and support is really important to me. Alongside that, I also feel it's crucial to stay informed and show solidarity with people in Gaza, who are enduring severe hardship. Both causes are about fostering empathy, care and consciousness in the communities I'm part of. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? I want to give a big shout out to Cologne, which had (and still has) a huge impact on my development as an artist. It's a very small, but tight-knit scene that formed me during my time there. A special mention goes to Gewölbe, which has influenced me both creatively and as a space where I could truly express and find myself over the years. Another remarkable experience for me has been Essence (FKA Goodness) in Copenhagen, an off-location party and collective with a very forward-thinking and inspiring community, where there's freedom in what you play and genuine respect for each other. You've spoken about mental health as an important cause. How has touring affected your view on it? I've been quite vulnerable to depression and anxiety since my youth, so the issue of mental health has always been part of my reality. Touring has, in some ways, helped me develop routines that are essential when you're in clubs every weekend. At the same time, when the pressure of expectations and the toll of travelling makes it difficult to access joy or lightness, everything can feel incredibly heavy. That's why I think it's so important that we support each other within the industry, show real understanding and build sustainable relationships. And even though it has been said so many times, it always comes down to something simple yet difficult—finding a balance. Your sound has shifted in recent years, from high-energy old-school rave to '00s tech house. What drove that change? First of all, thanks for asking! It's something I think about a lot, because for me being a DJ is always a process. I grew up in a household where electronic music was very present because my parents were ravers in the '90s. For example, when my mum picked me up from school, the car radio was almost always tuned to Fantasy Dance FM, which played a lot of trance. And since I've been digging and playing for almost six years now, I'm fascinated by many different genres. But I believe it really takes time until you can say you've found your own sound. Additionally, during the Covid-19 years, high BPMs and short-lived trends became very dominant. I realised that I couldn't stand the pace anymore. That's when my sound naturally shifted into something more minimalist and timeless. If you had to choose one artist or release that you feel best encapsulates your style, who or what would it be? That's such a tough question… but if I had to name one artist who really shaped me, it would be Asad Rizvi AKA Silverlining. Diving into old-school UK tech house in all its forms kind of changed my life and definitely influenced the way I approach my own sound.
translation.blurb: Sultry low-end grooves from a rising house enchantress.
